# Tools as Architecture Environments {#sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"We shape our tools and thereafter our tools shape us."*
>
> — John Culkin, *Saturday Review* (1967)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** John Culkin, an influential American media scholar, observed that our tools ultimately reshape our own behavior. In Architecture 2.0, any tool placed inside an AI-assisted architecture loop has to be treated as part of the loop contract. The wrapper shapes what actions are legal, what evidence is visible, and what can be rejected.
:::


```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 requires tools placed inside AI-assisted architecture loops to become environments, the executable, tool-facing part of a bounded architecture study's loop contract. This chapter treats ISA boundaries, compiler IRs, memory models, simulator APIs, profilers, RTL flows, EDA handoffs, runtimes, benchmarks, and telemetry systems as explicit places where automated systems and human reviewers can take design actions, observe feedback, log costs and constraints, and reject invalid or unsupported candidates.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What turns a tool flow into an environment a generative model can act in and a
reviewer can trust?
:::

@sec-data-representations-world-models argued that a loop can only act on what it represents. This chapter
asks where the loop acts and opens the design-loop card's environment field. In Architecture 1.0, tools often sit behind the
architect. Simulators, scripts, compilers, profilers, spreadsheets, RTL flows,
EDA tools, dashboards, and deployment logs are the means by which a human
expert gathers evidence. In Architecture 2.0, those same tools should be treated
as explicit environments when they sit inside AI-assisted design loops. They
define what actions are legal, what observations are returned, how expensive
feedback is, which assumptions are baked in, what state is logged, and what can
reject a candidate.

> **Architecture environment.** An architecture environment is the executable,
> tool-facing part of a bounded architecture study's loop contract. It specifies
> the legal actions,
> observations and feedback costs, logged state and provenance, rejection
> conditions, and failure or escalation paths for the tools a design loop acts
> through.

The loop contract is the study-level term @sec-architecture-20-ontology defines
beside the design loop, the full set of commitments a bounded study makes; the
environment contract this chapter develops is its tool-facing subset, the part a
harness can execute and enforce.

The boundary with the previous chapter is deliberate. A world model predicts or
constrains the consequences of actions. An environment defines which actions are
legal, what observations come back, what failures mean, and what evidence is
logged. A simulator, surrogate, or rule set can contribute to both, but the loop
must record which role it is playing before its output can support a claim.

To keep these boundaries clear, we must use the tool vocabulary carefully.

- A *tool* performs an operation.
- A *wrapper* calls that tool.
- An *environment contract* states what actions are legal, what observations
  return, what costs and failures mean, and what evidence is logged.
- A *harness* is the maintained runnable object around that contract, including
  workloads, versions, scripts, provenance, invalid-action records, and review
  state.

A benchmark harness makes the distinction concrete. The benchmark program is
not the environment by itself. The environment is the maintained bundle of
allowed inputs, run rules, scripts, versions, result checks, and disclosure
requirements that lets another team decide whether the run means the same
thing. MLPerf (@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales) shows this pattern; its
submission harnesses and reporting rules are what let another team make that
judgment [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. An Architecture
2.0 environment applies the same discipline to design actions, tool feedback,
and rejection records.

A tool wrapper is not plumbing. It is a
research claim about the architecture problem. It decides which parts of the
design space are visible, which constraints are enforceable, which metrics are
trusted, which failures are recorded, and which actions are silently
impossible. A weak environment can make a strong method look useful by hiding
the hard cases. A disciplined environment can make a modest method valuable by
making the task bounded, repeatable, and rejectable.

The same contract is what makes comparison meaningful. Fair method comparisons
require compatible, or explicitly mapped, workload definitions, action schemas,
evaluation costs, invalid-action rules, provenance records, and claim limits.
Otherwise a result may compare private worlds rather than architecture methods.

The lighthouse prompt makes the point concrete. The request, a low-power,
64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an XRBench-class real-time mobile XR
workload under a 3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP mobile process, is not one
call to one tool. It needs a workload harness, software stack, ISA and vector
assumptions, candidate architecture representation, simulator or estimator,
power model, compiler/runtime interface, validity checks, provenance log, and
a way to record rejected alternatives. If any of those pieces are implicit,
the prompt is not yet an Architecture 2.0 loop. It is only a sentence.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can turn a tool path into a reviewable environment
contract. That means you can:

- **Turn** a tool into an environment with explicit actions, observations, costs, invalid outcomes, and failure paths;
- **Read** a result by naming the environment that produced it;
- **Explain** why environment contracts make AI-method and paper comparisons meaningful;
- **Distinguish** illegal candidate actions from tool, setup, and harness failures;
- **Detect** proxy mismatch and proxy gaming, simulators included, before they mislead the loop;
- **Maintain** a comparable harness as workloads, tools, and action schemas change.
:::

## Tools Shape the Research Question

Computer architects have always used tools to reason quantitatively about
systems. Simulators, analytic models, profilers, compilers, RTL generators,
EDA flows, and measurement systems make design spaces tractable. They also
shape the questions that can be asked. A simulator with a particular memory
model makes some cache questions natural and others awkward. A compiler pass
that exposes one schedule representation and hides another constrains what an
optimizer can change. An EDA flow that returns timing and power after hours of
work makes feedback precious. A runtime telemetry system that reports
aggregate utilization but not per-request interference makes some deployment
claims difficult to support.
When tools are used by a human expert, some of these limits can remain tacit. For an AI-assisted
loop, tacit limits become hidden state. The loop can act safely only on
constraints, costs, and rejection rules the environment exposes.

The usual way to describe these tacit boundaries is to say that tools have limitations. That is true,
but too weak. Tools do not merely limit architecture work; they define its
observable world. They decide what state exists for the loop, what actions can
be applied to that state, and what feedback comes back. The classic quantitative tradition in computer architecture shifted the field from intuition to rigorous simulation and measurement [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].
Architecture 2.0 extends that tradition, asking for one more layer of
explicitness. Where the first era measured systems, the second era makes the measurement environment itself a programmable artifact. The tool interface itself must be part of the design object.

This level of explicitness matters because generative and learning-based methods are literal about
interfaces. A human architect can sometimes infer that a simulator result is
out of distribution, that a benchmark run used a stale configuration, or that
a reported improvement is not meaningful because the compiler changed. A
method acting through an environment will not infer those facts unless the
environment represents them. The environment must expose enough state for
useful action and enough constraints for safe rejection.

The right question is therefore not, "Which tool did the paper use?" The
better question is, "What environment did the loop define?" That question
forces the paper or project to name the contract fields. Once those are visible, method claims
become easier to compare.

## Interfaces Are Action Boundaries

A tool's interface makes its action and observation schema concrete.
Architecture is often described as the boundary between hardware and
software.
For Architecture 2.0, that statement has an operational meaning. Interfaces
are where actions become legal or illegal, observations become meaningful or
misleading, and evidence becomes portable or trapped inside one tool script.
None of these boundaries is just a convenience for implementation. Each one defines what a loop can change and what can reject the change, whether it is an ISA, a compiler IR, a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) such as SLICC for cache coherence, a memory model, an accelerator runtime, a simulator API, a benchmark harness, an EDA handoff, or a telemetry schema. When the claim depends on coherence, scheduling, memory semantics, or physical constraints, the loop should act through the interface or schema that owns those semantics. The corollary is that unstructured C++ or RTL becomes a review risk when it bypasses those modular contracts, not because it is invalid by default.

This is why tool interfaces belong in the architecture argument rather than in
an appendix. A generator that emits a schedule must know the schedule
language. A search method that changes memory hierarchy parameters must know
which combinations the simulator accepts and which violate a software-visible
contract. A critique system that reads a synthesis report must know which
warnings are fatal, which are informational, and which require a higher-fidelity
check. The interface is the boundary where method capability meets
architectural validity.

A credible loop often has to expose a set of interfaces
(@tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries). The table is not a complete taxonomy. Its
claim is that every interface has two jobs. It makes some actions possible,
and it defines what evidence those actions can produce. If either side is
hidden, an automated optimizer can appear capable while acting outside the architecture
problem the human intended to solve.

| **Interface** | **What it makes actionable** | **Evidence it makes interpretable** | **Failure if hidden** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ISA and vector contract | Instructions, registers, vector length, exceptions, privilege, and binary compatibility. | Correct execution, portability, software-visible behavior, and compatibility tests. | The loop proposes a microarchitecture that software cannot legally target. |
| Compiler IR and schedule representation | Lowering choices, tiling, fusion, layout, vectorization, and target-specific code generation. | Compiler success, generated code, performance counters, and optimization provenance. | Performance is attributed to hardware while the software contract changed. |
| Memory and coherence model | Ordering, sharing, cacheability, consistency, DMA, and synchronization assumptions. | Correctness tests, contention behavior, latency, bandwidth, and race or ordering failures. | A candidate looks fast because it violated the program's memory assumptions. |
| Accelerator or runtime API | Invocation, data movement, synchronization, library calls, queues, and resource ownership. | End-to-end latency, overhead, utilization, portability, and software integration cost. | Specialized hardware is efficient in isolation but unusable in the system. |
| Simulator or environment API | Legal parameters, workload inputs, observations, errors, seeds, and fidelity levels. | Comparable runs, replayable experiments, invalid-action records, and feedback cost. | The method optimizes simulator quirks or incomparable configurations. |
| EDA handoff and constraints | RTL, clocks, floorplan hints, timing constraints, power intent, physical limits, and signoff checks. | Timing, area, power, congestion, rule violations, and implementation feasibility. | A candidate survives architectural simulation but fails physical reality. |
| Benchmark harness | Inputs, versions, metrics, splits, data-leakage rules, and submission constraints. | Coverage, reproducibility, benchmark validity, and claim scope. | The loop overfits a stale or leaky benchmark slice. |
| Telemetry and deployment schema | Live workload mix, service-level objectives (SLOs), counters, interference, rollout state, and drift signals. | Field behavior, regressions, rollback triggers, and post-deployment calibration. | Production evidence is rich but too confounded to support the architecture claim. |

: **Architecture interfaces define action and evidence boundaries:** Each interface tells the loop what can be changed, what can be observed, what feedback is meaningful, and what can reject an invalid action. {#tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries tbl-colwidths="[20,25,25,20]"}

The EDA handoff row is worth making concrete, because it is where a wrapper does
the most work. Candidate validity comes in two tiers, and honest environments
separate them; environment validity, whether the harness still preserves the
question, is a different matter (@sec-archops-operating-discipline). *Schema
legality* is cheap and checkable up front, asking whether the
parameters, clocks, and interfaces are well formed. *Physical feasibility* is
late and expensive, because routability, timing closure, congestion, and IR drop
often surface only at synthesis or place-and-route. A late failure must map back
to the earlier action that caused it, not read as a contract violation.

Wrapping a commercial flow as an environment is therefore not a thin API. The
wrapper has to emit tool-specific scripts, parse unstructured reports, survive
timeouts and run-to-run nondeterminism, and attach the fidelity or confidence
label itself, because the tool emits metrics, warnings, and logs, not a fidelity
level. To stay auditable, its provenance record must carry more than a bare
number. It records the tool and host version, thread count, license state,
run-to-run variance, warnings and waivers, and a hash of the report the number
came from. Two "identical" runs that differ on any of these are not comparable,
and a loop that hides the difference will trust a result it should have rejected.

That realism also limits what Architecture 2.0 should try to standardize. The
field does not need a universal vendor API or an open substitute for every
foundry rule. It needs stable contract surfaces that let reviewers see what was
changed, what was observed, what stayed confidential, and what could reject the
claim, while leaving proprietary implementation details protected
(@tbl-standardizable-proprietary-contract).

| **Contract layer** | **Can be standardized** | **Usually remains proprietary** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Action schema | Task identifiers, legal edit classes, invalid-action categories, and stage labels. | Vendor command syntax, internal scripts, and product-specific flow recipes. |
| Observation schema | Metric names, fidelity labels, report hashes, warning classes, and failure categories. | Full report bodies, foundry rule details, and confidential workload traces. |
| Feedback record | Tool/version hashes, seed records, queue or license state, elapsed time, and artifact pointers. | License-server behavior, internal tool telemetry, and nonpublic debug artifacts. |
| Stop and escalation interface | Gate type, reviewer role, escalation condition, waiver status, and claim level blocked. | Product review details, customer commitments, waiver rationale, and named approvers. |
| Disclosure boundary | Redaction reason, disclosure tier, reviewer authority, and attestation handle. | Raw PDK contents, proprietary RTL, private baselines, and customer data. |

: **Closed flows can still expose review contracts:** Architecture 2.0 should converge on records, field names, and review semantics around commercial EDA and foundry flows where possible, without prescribing vendor implementations, foundry-rule semantics, signoff meaning, or protected design state. {#tbl-standardizable-proprietary-contract tbl-colwidths="[18,34,38]"}

The lighthouse prompt then shows why the split matters. One request crosses
ISA, workload, physical design, software, and evidence surfaces, turning the
standardization question into an interface test.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="One prompt, many interfaces"}
**Context.** The lighthouse request crosses multiple interfaces at once, so no
single simulator API or wrapper is the environment.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** "64-bit RISC-V-based" is an ISA and ABI/software
contract. The fragment "vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block" is an
action boundary over compute organization and integration. "XRBench-class
real-time mobile XR workload" fixes the workload harness and quality-of-service
target. "3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP mobile process" invokes power and
physical-design constraints.

**Boundary.** When the optimizer proposes a SoC block instead of an isolated accelerator, it changes the action space. The environment must now expose memory attachment, coherence, interrupts, DMA, runtime ownership, verification scope, and the evidence needed to show that the subsystem works in the larger system. Otherwise, the loop will optimize blindly.

**Takeaway.** The environment is a bundle of interfaces whose contracts must
reject software-incompatible, physically implausible, or integration-breaking
changes.
:::

In practice, concrete tools instantiate this bundle of interfaces at different fidelity levels. The point
is not that Architecture 2.0 requires one canonical simulator or one vendor
flow. The point is that a loop must say which environment it is acting in, what
that environment can observe, and what authority its feedback has (@tbl-environment-instances).

| **Environment instance** | **Action boundary** | **Feedback and evidence** | **Loop lesson** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cycle-level or full-system simulation (e.g., gem5) [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5] | Core, cache, memory-system, ISA, and workload configuration. | Statistics, traces, timing-model behavior, simulator warnings, and scoped performance comparisons. | Strong for controlled DSE; bounded by model fidelity and configuration state. |
| Compiled RTL simulation (e.g., Verilator) [@Veripool2026Verilator] | RTL modules, test benches, assertions, generated C++/SystemC models, and debug hooks. | Functional behavior, waveform/debug evidence, assertion failures, and implementation-adjacent traces. | Moves closer to implementation but narrows throughput and increases debug cost. |
| FPGA-accelerated simulation (e.g., FireSim) [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim] | RTL target, workload image, network model, FPGA mapping, and runtime configuration. | Faster cycle-exact feedback, workload-scale behavior, instrumented counters, and deployment-like experiments. | Speed changes sample economics, but setup and observability become part of the evidence. |
| Synthesis, place-and-route, and signoff flow [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign; @BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing] | RTL, constraints, floorplan hints, clocks, power intent, libraries, and process assumptions. | Area, timing, power, congestion, rule violations, waived warnings, and closure failures. | High-fidelity samples are scarce; use them as rejection gates, not blind search targets. |

: **Concrete tools instantiate the environment contract:** A credible loop should name the simulator, RTL flow, emulation path, EDA stage, or deployment harness it is acting through, because each one exposes different actions, observations, costs, and failure paths. {#tbl-environment-instances tbl-colwidths="[22,24,26,18]"}

For each row, the Architecture 2.0 object is not the tool name but the
maintained action/observation record: workload version, legal edits, failed
runs, waivers, escalation condition, and accountable owner.

In practice these environments are rarely used in isolation. Open frameworks
integrate them. Chipyard, an open-source framework for generating and evaluating
RISC-V systems-on-chip, wraps a configuration system, Chisel-based RTL
generators (Chisel is a hardware construction language) such as the Rocket and
BOOM cores and the Hwacha vector unit, and a FIRRTL compiler path (FIRRTL is its
intermediate representation).
It ties these to the simulation, synthesis, and FPGA-emulation backends the
table lists, so one configuration is carried reproducibly from a generated
design to each evaluation path (@fig-chipyard-framework) [@chipyard].
For an AI-assisted loop, the useful property is where the
loop actually runs. The automated optimizer acts at the configuration boundary, editing one
configuration under a set of legal actions; each backend, whether simulation,
synthesis, or FPGA emulation, returns an observation record; and those records,
together with invalid-action logs, escalation thresholds, and accountable decision points, are
the loop contract that keeps a generated configuration from becoming an unowned
commitment.

![**An integrated environment ties the backends together:** A reproducible SoC generation framework connects a configuration system, RTL generators, and a compiler to simulation, synthesis, and FPGA-emulation backends, so one configuration reaches every evaluation path.](images/F6b-chipyard-framework){#fig-chipyard-framework width="100%" fig-alt="Flow diagram in which a configuration system feeds Chisel RTL generators (Rocket, BOOM, Hwacha) and a FIRRTL compiler, which fan out to RTL simulation, VLSI synthesis, and FPGA emulation backends."}

However, even the broad categories in @tbl-environment-instances hide internal ladders of fidelity. Post-synthesis timing, for
example, is often a useful surrogate for post-route timing, but routing
congestion, IR drop, clocking, and signoff checks can still reject a candidate
that looked acceptable at synthesis. The lesson is not that every loop must
begin with the strongest tool. It is that the loop must know which feedback is
a proxy and which later check has authority to overturn it.
For an AI-assisted loop, that later authority is what decides whether a candidate
earns another sample, an escalation, or termination.

Physical-design environments make this staged contract particularly visible. A loop should
not report "EDA feedback" as if all tool stages had the same authority.
Common stages differ in what they can and cannot
reject (@tbl-eda-stage-contract).

| **EDA stage** | **Feedback returned** | **What it can reject** | **What it cannot prove alone** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Logic synthesis | Mapped gates, area proxy, timing estimates, constraint warnings, and early power estimates. | Nonsynthesizable RTL, impossible constraints, obvious area/timing problems. | Routability, final timing, IR drop, clocking, and post-layout power. |
| Floorplanning and placement | Cell locations, utilization, congestion hints, timing pressure, and macro or memory placement effects. | Floorplans that create severe congestion, timing pressure, or integration problems. | Final routed timing, signoff power, manufacturability, and workload-level benefit. |
| Clock, routing, and power closure | Routed timing, clock behavior, congestion, design-rule checks, power integrity, and closure failures. | Candidates whose physical effects invalidate earlier architecture estimates. | Product commitment without verification, workload coverage, and architecture review. |
| Signoff and review | Tool signoff reports, waivers, residual risks, review decisions, and ownership records. | Unsupported claims, unacceptable waived warnings, and evidence gaps before commitment. | Generality beyond the stated workload, process, tool version, and design context. |

: **EDA feedback has stage-specific authority:** From logic synthesis through
signoff, each physical-design stage returns different evidence and should be
allowed to reject different claims. {#tbl-eda-stage-contract tbl-colwidths="[18,26,25,21]"}

This arrangement is not hypothetical. Some commercial physical-implementation
flows already treat synthesis and place-and-route as a search environment, where tool
directives and floorplan parameters become actions, full tool runs become
transitions, and reports become observations about power, performance, area, and
closure [@Synopsys2023DSOai; @Cadence2021Cerebrus]. The durable point is not
which vendor leads, since the products will be renamed and replaced. It is that
an EDA flow becomes an environment only when those directives are an action
schema, reports are observations with provenance, closure and signoff are
allowed to reject specified candidate states, and the accountable team owns the
review rules the loop may not bypass.

## The Architecture Environment Abstraction

To formalize these interfaces across different tools, we need an abstraction. The environment has an executable core, but it is not only software. It receives
a proposed action, checks whether that action is meaningful, calls one or more
tools, collects observations, logs provenance, and returns feedback that can
become evidence. It can also enforce or record declarative interface contracts:
ISA rules, memory models, telemetry schemas, benchmark rules, and EDA
constraints. An architecture environment fundamentally reshapes this relationship (@fig-environment-tool-interface). Rather than just taking inputs and returning outputs, it wraps the underlying tool with a strict contract that exposes legal actions, bounds observations, enforces physical costs, and explicitly defines rejection paths for invalid states.

![**A tool becomes an environment when its contract is explicit:** A credible environment binds a bounded study and workload to legal actions, observations, constraints, cost, provenance, feedback, invalid-action semantics, and rejection paths.](images/F6-environment-tool-interface){#fig-environment-tool-interface width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram of a tool wrapper becoming an architecture environment by binding a bounded study and workload to legal actions, observations, costs, provenance, feedback, and rejection paths."}

The figure is a loop contract, not a software architecture diagram. Each
component is useful only after the environment names the action it permits, the
feedback it returns, and which outcomes that feedback may reject. That
is why two projects can use the same simulator but define different architecture
environments.

The abstraction is a contract. The environment is the maintained interface around tool calls, legal actions, observations, failures, costs, provenance, and rejection paths. An environment may expose a scalar objective to a method, but it should not collapse the architecture claim into that scalar. The contract must preserve raw observations and cost telemetry, such as cycle counts, gate area, test failures, invalid actions, and stronger rejection checks. Otherwise the loop invites reward hacking[^fn-ml-reward-hacking], where a method optimizes for simulator bugs instead of physical reality. The contract requires that the loop make a small set of obligations explicit (@tbl-environment-contract). These eight fields are what the design-loop card's environment field points to when a study is reviewed.

[^fn-ml-reward-hacking]: The proxy-gaming section later in this chapter defines reward hacking in full, with the simulator case and its machine-learning-safety source.

| **Field** | **What it defines** | **Question it answers** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Workload distribution** | Inputs, traces, benchmark versions, software stack, and operating scenarios. | What behavior is the design supposed to serve? |
| **Action schema** | Parameters, edits, configurations, generated artifacts, or commands the loop may propose. | What can the method actually change? |
| **Observation schema** | Metrics, traces, logs, reports, errors, and artifacts returned after an action. | What can the loop observe after acting? |
| **Constraints and validity** | Type checks, feasibility rules, physical/manufacturing limits, software ecosystem compatibility, and invalid-action handling. | What makes a candidate illegal before performance is considered? |
| **Evaluation cost** | Cost, latency, fidelity, determinism, parallel scalability, and sample limits for each observation source. | How often can the loop call this tool path? |
| **Provenance record** | Tool versions, seeds, inputs, configuration files, assumptions, and artifact hashes. | Could a human audit the result and locate the runnable inputs when replay is claimed? |
| **Supply-chain and integrity record** | Dependency, dataset, model, prompt, tool-binary, receipt, and attestation identifiers. | Can the loop tell whether the evidence source changed or was tampered with? |
| **Failure and review paths** | Conditions that invalidate, stop, revise, or escalate a candidate, separated from tool and setup failures. | What happens after each outcome? |

: **An environment contract makes tool use auditable:** The loop should state its action schema, observations, costs, failures, provenance, and human-review boundaries before methods operate inside it. {#tbl-environment-contract tbl-colwidths="[22,32,34]"}

The contract is deliberately broader than reinforcement learning terminology.
Actions, observations, and rewards are useful terms, but architecture work
also needs constraints, invalid-action semantics, provenance, and human
decision points. A loop that proposes a cache size, vector width, chiplet
partition, compiler flag, or RTL edit needs to know not only whether a score
improved, but whether the candidate is legal, traceable, comparable, and worth
escalating to a higher-fidelity evaluation.

Applying this contract to the lighthouse prompt, the environment might expose actions such as
changing vector width, local memory size, issue width, cache configuration,
accelerator interface, or data layout. It might return observations such as
latency, throughput, estimated power, memory traffic, area proxy, simulator
warnings, compiler failures, and rejected workloads. It should also return
cost, tracking how long the run took, which fidelity level was used, and how much
confidence the loop should place in the feedback. Without that cost and
provenance, the loop cannot reason about sample efficiency or evidence.

## ArchGym as a Worked Example

ArchGym, a project from the author's group, makes the environment idea
concrete in the architecture domain. It frames bounded architecture tasks as
gym-style environments that automated methods act in through defined
interfaces [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. The name is
deliberate. It borrows from OpenAI Gym, which standardized the agent-environment
interface in reinforcement learning. Just as one learning algorithm can drive
many Gym environments, an ArchGym user can swap a memory-controller simulator for
an accelerator simulator while keeping the same optimization or learning method
intact, which is exactly the composability a shared environment is supposed to
buy. The
Architecture 2.0 gymnasium essay makes the same broader argument. The field
needs data-centric environments where architecture tasks, feedback, and
evaluation are exposed systematically [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2023Gymnasium].
A community workshop later turned that argument into a broader field discussion
[@Architecture20CommunityWorkshop]. Read through this chapter's lens, the
SIGARCH outcome report's agenda around datasets, algorithms, tools and
infrastructure, best practices, workforce training, and industry collaboration
is also an environment-contract agenda. Its trust and training threads ask
whether evidence can be made legible and whether the next architect can learn to
produce it [@Architecture20SIGARCHWorkshopOutcomes].

For our purposes, the important lesson is not that every project should use ArchGym literally.
The lesson is that a shared environment changes the research question. Instead
of asking whether one optimizer beat another under a private script, the
community can ask which task was defined, which action space was exposed, what
feedback was available, what workloads were used, and which tools or methods
were compared under the same conditions. That makes method claims less
anecdotal. ArchGym's own headline experiment makes the stakes concrete. Run
inside the same environments under equal sample budgets, five families of
search algorithms converged to designs of comparable quality
(@fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget). At the small budgets a slow simulator can
actually afford, even a random walker matches or beats the learned methods, and
reinforcement learning pulls ahead only once samples grow plentiful; the same
study reports up to a 90 percent interquartile spread in outcome across
hyperparameter choices for this task. Differences that private setups would
have reported as method wins traced instead to the setup, budget, and
hyperparameter lottery rather than the method [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym].

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Equal budgets dissolve method wins:** Mean normalized reward (higher is better) reached by five search-algorithm families inside the same ArchGym DRAM memory-controller environment at equal simulator-sample budgets [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Whiskers reproduce the published variability extents, and they overlap across families at every budget. Values are digitized transcriptions of the paper's Figure 7(a) at its plotted resolution; reinforcement learning shows no bar at the two smallest budgets in the source.
#| out-width: "88%"
#| fig-alt: "Grouped bar chart with error whiskers showing mean normalized reward for five search-algorithm families, ACO, BO, GA, RL, and RW, at simulator-sample budgets from one hundred to two hundred fifty thousand in the ArchGym DRAM memory-controller environment. Whisker intervals overlap across families at every budget. The random walker is among the strongest families at the middle budgets, while reinforcement learning has no bar at the two smallest budgets, is near zero at ten thousand samples, and leads only at the largest budget."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter5-archgym-equal-budget.csv
# ArchGym ISCA 2023 [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym], Fig. 7(a): mean normalized
# reward vs. simulator-sample budget for DRAM memory-controller design
# (DRAMGym, DRAMSys). Means and whisker extents are programmatically
# digitized from the published figure's native-resolution raster (arXiv
# 2306.08888v1 PDF, accessed 2026-07-13; axis-calibration residual <= 0.002
# reward units, reading uncertainty ~ +/-0.003). RL bars are absent in the
# source at the 100 and 1,000-sample budgets and are omitted, not zeroed.
budgets = ["100", "1K", "10K", "100K", "250K"]
agents = [
    ("ACO", COLORS["blue"]),
    ("BO", COLORS["orange"]),
    ("GA", COLORS["green"]),
    ("RL", COLORS["red"]),
    ("RW", COLORS["purple"]),
]
# (mean, whisker low, whisker high) per budget; None = not plotted in source
dram = {
    "ACO": [(0.026, 0.017, 0.037), (0.034, 0.024, 0.046), (0.045, 0.033, 0.058),
            (0.030, 0.021, 0.041), (0.051, 0.039, 0.065)],
    "BO": [(0.046, 0.025, 0.064), (0.112, 0.042, 0.182), (0.112, 0.042, 0.182),
           (0.112, 0.042, 0.182), (0.115, 0.048, 0.182)],
    "GA": [(0.043, 0.025, 0.070), (0.083, 0.053, 0.110), (0.098, 0.070, 0.124),
           (0.097, 0.062, 0.124), (0.107, 0.081, 0.125)],
    "RL": [None, None, (0.007, 0.003, 0.013),
           (0.111, 0.062, 0.208), (0.154, 0.134, 0.168)],
    "RW": [(0.027, 0.019, 0.032), (0.027, 0.014, 0.037), (0.132, 0.112, 0.153),
           (0.132, 0.117, 0.147), (0.132, 0.121, 0.142)],
}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.7, 2.25))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.105, right=0.985, top=0.83, bottom=0.19)

width = 0.15
for k, (name, color) in enumerate(agents):
    xs, ys, err_lo, err_hi = [], [], [], []
    for i, v in enumerate(dram[name]):
        if v is None:
            continue
        xs.append(i + (k - 2) * width)
        ys.append(v[0])
        err_lo.append(v[0] - v[1])
        err_hi.append(v[2] - v[0])
    ax.bar(xs, ys, width=width * 0.9, color=color, zorder=2, label=name)
    ax.errorbar(xs, ys, yerr=[err_lo, err_hi], fmt="none",
                ecolor=COLORS["ink"], elinewidth=0.7, capsize=1.4,
                capthick=0.7, zorder=3)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(budgets)))
ax.set_xticklabels(budgets)
ax.set_xlabel("simulator-sample budget", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("mean normalized reward", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.23)
ax.set_yticks([0.0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
fig.legend(loc="upper center", ncol=5, fontsize=6.2, frameon=False,
           bbox_to_anchor=(0.53, 1.0), handlelength=1.0, columnspacing=1.2)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Whether a reported difference is a method win or a budget artifact is a cheap
question inside a shared gym and an expensive one outside it. When the setups
are private and the stakes are production silicon, the same question hardens
into a dispute.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Two teams, two private setups"}
**The claim.** The AlphaChip dispute, concerning a reinforcement-learning method for macro placement, is at bottom a story about missing shared conditions. The original Google team and the independent groups that later reassessed the result did not disagree only about a number. They ran the method in different setups, on different test cases, with different baselines and compute budgets, and each read the result through its own script [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The gap.** When two parties compare under private, non-shared setups, there is no way to separate a real difference from a difference in conditions. Workload IDs, action schemas, tool versions, seeds, compute budgets, baseline harnesses, and post-route checks all become part of the claim. The authors could argue the critics had not run the method as published, and the critics could argue the method did not beat a strong baseline, and both could be right about their own environment. The setup ambiguity made the algorithm comparison hard to interpret.

**The lesson.** Fix and share those conditions, and the comparison becomes a question about the method rather than the setup. That is what a shared environment buys. A claim is only as credible as the conditions under which it can be replayed.
:::

The comparison is credible only when every AI-assisted system acts against
compatible, or explicitly mapped, represented state, feedback records,
invalid-action and environment-failure rules, escalation policy, and
human-review rules. The same terms let us read ArchGym against the contract,
mapping its rows onto the fields of @tbl-environment-contract at a research
gym's granularity (@tbl-archgym-mini-contract). Task and limits bound the claim, provenance
folds into the versioned tool path, and the supply-chain and integrity record
is the one contract field such a gym typically lacks.

| **Contract field** | **ArchGym-style instance** |
| --- | --- |
| **Task** | Search or tune a bounded architecture design space under a declared workload and metric. |
| **Action schema** | Architecture parameters, simulator knobs, or mapping choices the automated method is allowed to change. |
| **Observation and reward** | Metrics returned by the simulator or tool path, with the reward derived from those observations. |
| **Workload and tool path** | Benchmark inputs, simulator configuration, and versioned evaluation scripts. |
| **Action and outcome handling** | Rules for rejected parameters and unsupported candidate states, with separate records for failed runs, timeouts, tool crashes, and other environment failures. |
| **Evaluation cost** | Approximate turnaround and sample budget for each evaluation regime. |
| **Failure and review paths** | Independent checks that can reject a result, environment failures that require repair, and the strongest claim this setup can support. |
| **Limits** | Simplified simulators, cleaner action spaces, and missing industrial constraints can bound what the result proves. |

: **A worked environment example is a contract, not only a benchmark:** ArchGym is useful for Architecture 2.0 because it makes the task, action space, feedback, comparison setting, and limits discussable. {#tbl-archgym-mini-contract tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

ArchGym also shows why the environment chapter cannot be collapsed into the
methods chapter. Once an environment is defined, many methods can interact
with it: Bayesian optimization, reinforcement learning, evolutionary search[^fn-ml-evolutionary-search],
surrogate-guided exploration[^fn-ml-surrogate-guided], random search, heuristic search, or a human
designer using the environment as an instrumented assistant. The environment
is the common ground on which method comparisons become meaningful.

[^fn-ml-evolutionary-search]: In this chapter, a way to propose candidate actions by mutation, crossover, and selection, useful only when the environment can reject invalid or weak candidates.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-guided]: In this chapter, using a fast predictor to screen candidate actions while preserving the stronger feedback needed to reject proxy wins.

At the same time, ArchGym should not be treated as if it solves the whole
Architecture 2.0 problem. A gym can still use simplified simulators. It may
not include proprietary physical-design constraints, confidential workloads,
tool-license behavior, workload drift, failed-run capture, or deployment
telemetry. Its action spaces may be cleaner than industrial design spaces. Its
feedback may be faster and more standardized than the feedback available in
late-stage silicon work. Those limitations are not reasons to dismiss the
environment pattern. They are reasons to make environment validity a first-class
concern.

## Interfaces Make Loops Composable

Validity is not the only reason to insist on an explicit interface. A single
tool wrapper can support one experiment. A disciplined interface can
support a research ecosystem. The difference is composability. If action
schemas, observation schemas, workloads, provenance records, and validity
rules are explicit, then generators, predictors, optimizers, critics,
verifiers, and human reviewers can be swapped or combined without rewriting
the whole loop.

This is how architecture environments can become community infrastructure. As
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales argued for benchmark governance, useful
infrastructure such as MLPerf does more than name workloads. It creates rules,
versions, metrics, submissions, and comparison conventions that help a community
interpret results [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. Benchmark governance governs
comparisons; environment governance must also govern what AI-assisted systems may try, which
actions are invalid, what feedback means, and what can stop the loop. A useful
environment should not merely publish a script; it should publish the contract
under which actions are legal, observations are valid, and evidence can be
compared.
Keeping those contracts valid after the first paper is the lifecycle practice
@sec-archops-operating-discipline develops.

This transition from single-use wrappers to shared community infrastructure is why our opening vocabulary reserved the word harness. A multi-participant harness adds role
boundaries, specifying which component may propose, which may execute, which may critique,
which may verify, and which accountable decision is required before escalation. The
distinction matters because a wrapper can automate one experiment, but a
harness can accumulate reusable knowledge about a design space.

A minimal implementation record for such a harness should be deliberately
ordinary. It can include the task identifier, workload version, input
distribution, action fields, read-only constraints, tool commands, observation
fields, fidelity level, runtime cost, random seed, tool versions, generated
artifacts, failure status, rejection reason, and accountable decision.

That list is intentionally mundane. Mundane records are what make loops
auditable. If a method proposes an architecture candidate, the environment
should preserve not only the winning score but also the command that produced
it, the workload revision, the tool version, the failed alternatives, the
warnings, and the conditions under which the candidate would be rejected.

This focus on composability also changes how we interpret AI-assisted systems. A compound
design system may have a planner, code generator, simulator caller, surrogate
model, evidence critic, and human reviewer, components that instantiate the
roles above at one system's granularity. Those components can coordinate
only if the environment gives them a shared state representation and stable
interfaces. If those components are implemented as several agents, the need for
an environment contract does not shrink; it grows. The harness has to record
which component read which state, which action it took, which feedback it used,
and which gate could reject its output. Without that, an "agent" is merely a
wrapper around a pile of scripts. With it, the loop can become an inspectable
system.

## Feedback Latency and Fidelity

An inspectable loop still has to price what it inspects. An environment contract
must expose the cost and fidelity of its observations.
An analytic proxy may return in milliseconds, a cycle-level simulation may take
minutes or hours, and physical implementation or silicon measurements may
require days to months. If these differences remain hidden, a fast method can
overwhelm an expensive tool path or compare measurements that do not mean the
same thing.

Feedback regimes differ by orders of magnitude in turnaround and in how many
samples a loop can afford, and two of them, cycle-level simulation and physical
feedback, are pinned with measured anchors from published tool reports
(@fig-fidelity-cost-confidence). The ranges themselves
are not universal measurements, and higher cost does not make an observation
automatically authoritative. The requirement here is narrower: label the
tool stage, version, elapsed cost, fidelity, raw observations, and outcome
class, and map a failed run back to the action that caused it when possible.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization uses those fields to choose
methods; @sec-feedback-verification-trust decides what evidence can support a
stronger claim.

The cost structure is a multi-fidelity problem familiar in computational
science, where many cheap observations are combined with fewer expensive ones
to make optimization and uncertainty quantification affordable
[^fn-stats-uncertainty-quantification]
[@PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity]. An architecture environment
does not need to prescribe the escalation policy here. It must make the
observation sources and their costs distinguishable enough for later policy and
review.

[^fn-stats-uncertainty-quantification]: In this chapter, uncertainty quantification means recording where an environment's observations are uncertain or unsupported; @sec-feedback-verification-trust develops the evidence consequence.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fidelity-cost-confidence
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Feedback regimes have different costs and sample budgets:** Low-cost feedback can screen many candidates, while implementation-adjacent observations are scarce. The ordering shows representative turnaround and acquisition cost, not automatic authority or commitment; each check still needs a declared scope and validation contract. Ink diamonds add published measured anchors. FireSim simulates 1,024 nodes cycle-exactly at 3.4 MHz, under 1,000 times slower than the 3.2 GHz targets it models, where single-server software simulators run at 5 to 100 KIPS [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]. A macro-placement re-evaluation measured 11 hours per design for simulated annealing and 38 to 55 hours for the reinforcement-learning flow, against under 12 hours of expert effort, for one physical-design stage evaluated through a commercial post-route flow [@ChengEtAl2023MacroPlacement].
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Horizontal range plot comparing representative observation latencies and shrinking sample budgets for feedback regimes from cheap proxies to silicon deployment, without treating cost or fidelity as automatic authority. Ink diamond markers add measured anchors, a FireSim cycle-exact simulation point at just under one thousand times real time on the cycle-level simulation row, and macro-placement turnaround points from eleven to fifty-five hours per design on the physical-feedback row."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, draw_range_rows, row_axis, top_log_axis

rows = [
    {"display_label": "analytic proxy / surrogate", "display_note": "thousands to millions of checks", "low_seconds": 0.001, "high_seconds": 1, "right_label": "screen candidates", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "trace / profile / replay", "display_note": "tens to thousands of slices", "low_seconds": 1, "high_seconds": 3600, "right_label": "characterize workload", "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "cycle-level simulation", "display_note": "tens to hundreds of comparisons", "low_seconds": 60, "high_seconds": 86400, "right_label": "compare candidates", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "compiler / runtime measurement", "display_note": "many local measurements; fewer portable claims", "low_seconds": 1, "high_seconds": 3600, "right_label": "validate software path", "color": COLORS["purple"]},
    {"display_label": "RTL / synthesis / physical feedback", "display_note": "handfuls to tens of scarce samples", "low_seconds": 3600, "high_seconds": 1_209_600, "right_label": "implementation evidence", "color": COLORS["red"]},
    {"display_label": "prototype / silicon / deployment", "display_note": "few deployment observations plus telemetry", "low_seconds": 604_800, "high_seconds": 31_536_000, "right_label": "deployment evidence", "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.85, 3.25))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.39, right=0.77, top=0.86, bottom=0.16)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(5e-4, 7e7),
    xticks=[1e-3, 1, 3600, 604800, 31536000],
    xticklabels=["1 ms", "1 s", "1 h", "1 wk", "1 yr"],
    xlabel="representative observation latency",
)
draw_range_rows(ax, rows, low_key="low_seconds", high_key="high_seconds", label_x=-0.90, right_x=1.03, label_fontsize=6.5, note_fontsize=5.3, right_fontsize=5.9)

# Measured anchors (source receipt:
# data/source-receipts/chapter5-measured-feedback-anchors.csv).
# FireSim ISCA 2018 [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]: 1,024-node cycle-exact
# simulation at 3.4 MHz vs. 3.2 GHz targets, "less than 1,000x slowdown over
# real-time" -> ~941 s to observe one second of target execution; the paper
# also gives per-node 10s-100s MHz and 5-100 KIPS for single-server software
# simulators (rates only, cited in the caption, no latency marker).
# Cheng et al. ISPD 2023 [@ChengEtAl2023MacroPlacement], Table IV, CT-Ariane:
# macro placement evaluated through a commercial post-route flow, SA 11.13 h,
# RL 37.89-55.16 h, human expert <12 h per design. Accessed 2026-07-13.
# The Verilator anchor is dropped: its only absolute measured rate is outside
# the bibliography.
firesim_x = 3.2e9 / 3.4e6  # ~941 s per second of target execution
sa_x = 11.13 * 3600
rl_lo_x, rl_hi_x = 37.89 * 3600, 55.16 * 3600
# The RL 38-55 h range is anchored by ink diamonds at both endpoints so every
# measured anchor uses the same diamond vocabulary as the note box.
ax.scatter([firesim_x, sa_x, rl_lo_x, rl_hi_x], [2, 4, 4, 4], marker="D",
           s=11, color=COLORS["ink"], zorder=4)
ax.text(firesim_x, 2.34,
        "FireSim, 1,024 nodes, cycle-exact:\n<1,000$\\times$ real time",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["muted"],
        linespacing=1.15, clip_on=False)
ax.text(3.2e4, 4.34,
        "macro placement, measured:\nSA 11 h, RL 38$-$55 h, expert <12 h",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["muted"],
        linespacing=1.15, clip_on=False)

add_note_box(fig, "Representative ranges only; ink diamonds mark published measured anchors; authority depends on scope, validation, and the decision contract.", xywh=(0.13, 0.035, 0.74, 0.085), fontsize=5.5)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The measured anchors show the top of that spread is not hypothetical. One
post-route evaluation already consumes eleven to fifty-five hours of tool time
per design, which is why the physical-feedback rows budget handfuls of samples
rather than thousands.

The sample-cost inventory in @tbl-sample-cost-regimes becomes an environment
checklist once each observation source also carries what the harness must
record and how it distinguishes a candidate outcome from an environment failure
(@tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity).

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

| **Observation source** | **Cost / latency intuition** | **Environment record** | **Outcome distinction** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Analytic proxy or learned surrogate | Milliseconds to seconds; many checks are possible. | Model and version, input domain, raw estimate, uncertainty, and calibration state. | Out-of-support or uncalibrated proxy versus a candidate that misses a declared bound. |
| Trace, profile, or replay | Seconds to hours depending on capture, replay, and privacy filtering. | Trace origin, workload and software version, slice rule, coverage, and redactions. | Stale or incomplete trace versus behavior that fails on a valid trace. |
| Cycle-level simulation | Minutes to days depending on detail and workload. | Simulator and configuration, seed, warm-up rule, statistics, warnings, and elapsed time. | Illegal candidate state versus simulator crash, timeout, or setup failure. |
| Compiler/runtime measurement | Build and profiling may take seconds to hours; target replay or tuning may take longer. | Toolchain, flags, generated code, runtime path, tests, counters, and host state. | Candidate-caused compile or correctness failure versus broken build infrastructure. |
| RTL, synthesis, or physical feedback | Hours to weeks as timing, power, congestion, and signoff enter. | EDA stage, constraints, libraries, reports, warnings, waivers, host state, and hashes. | Candidate violation versus license, queue, timeout, or tool failure. |
| Prototype, silicon, or deployment telemetry | High setup cost; field observations may span weeks or years. | Hardware revision, rollout state, telemetry schema, workload mix, incidents, and collection gaps. | Candidate regression versus deployment or measurement failure. |

: **Observation sources need distinct cost and failure semantics:** Representative ranges are project-dependent. The environment records enough context to compare observations and to separate candidate outcomes from harness failures. {#tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity tbl-colwidths="[19,22,32,25]"}

Lower-fidelity observations can still be gamed or contradicted. When a proxy
says "yes" and a stronger tool path says "no," the environment should retain
the mismatch rather than average it away. The simulator case below shows why.

Compiler and runtime feedback carry their own version of this same risk. A
hardware candidate can look weak because the schedule, tiling, vectorization,
layout, or runtime path is poor, not because the architecture is poor. That is
a false negative created by the software side of the environment. For the
lighthouse prompt, "vector-capable" therefore cannot mean only that an ISA
feature exists; the loop must also expose whether the compiler and runtime can
generate a credible path to use it.

Choosing a search strategy under these costs, including active
learning[^fn-ml-active-learning], belongs to
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization. Deciding when an observation
supports a stronger claim belongs to @sec-feedback-verification-trust. The
environment handoff is the typed observation, its cost and provenance, and its
failure classification.

[^fn-ml-active-learning]: Active learning selects which scarce simulator, synthesis, human, or deployment observation to request next [@Settles2009ActiveLearning]. The method chapter owns that selection policy; this chapter requires the environment to expose the selectable actions and record the resulting call.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Proxy Gaming and the Simulator Case

Because low-fidelity feedback can mislead a loop, an architecture environment must also defend itself against its own
abstractions. Simulators, analytical models, profilers, and compiler cost
models are not neutral oracles. They encode workload choices, warm-up rules,
timing models, memory-system assumptions, compiler defaults, branch predictor
state, cache initialization, interconnect models, and sampling choices. A
method that searches aggressively can discover weaknesses in those assumptions
just as easily as it can discover a good architecture candidate.

When the proxy is a simulator, *proxy mismatch* is the divergence between what a
cheap stand-in metric rewards and what the architectural objective requires.
When a method optimizes against that stand-in, the mismatch can produce the
failure described by Goodhart's law, in which a measure becomes unreliable as a
target [@Goodhart1975MonetaryManagement; @Strathern1997Goodhart]. Here it takes the specific form of a gap
between the behavior an environment reports and the behavior that would matter at
the next stronger fidelity level, such as a more detailed simulator, synthesis,
physical design, emulation, silicon, or deployment. The failure mode is not
merely an inaccurate number. It is a loop that learns the wrong lesson. A
mapping optimizer may exploit a memory model that omits contention. A compiler
autotuner may win by relying on a backend assumption that changes under a
different target. A hardware generator may improve a cycle-level metric while
creating timing, congestion, or verification problems that the current
environment cannot see. For example, an automated memory partitioner might find
a partition that hides latency in the simulator's memory model, only for
place-and-route to expose severe congestion when thousands of wires must cross
the die, preventing timing closure. A workload harness may reward one benchmark
version while hiding drift in the software stack.
A run that does not crash is not the same as hardware validity; the environment has
to define which illegal states, unsupported configurations, and silent
out-of-model behaviors it can actually reject.

A common failure pattern is not that the tool is useless. The loop can learn
exactly what the tool rewards. This is a local instance of proxy mismatch, the
architecture version of what machine-learning safety calls *specification
gaming* or *reward hacking* [@AmodeiEtAl2016ConcreteProblems]. A candidate can
improve a proxy by choosing an unsupported parameter corner, relying on a stale
workload slice, or shifting cost into a compiler/runtime path the current
harness does not measure. The environment should make that failure visible
through invalid-action checks, baseline replay, sensitivity tests, and
escalation to a stronger feedback source. @sec-feedback-verification-trust
treats proxy mismatch and its calibration defenses in full.

The adversarial version is sharper. A loop may be pressured through prompt
injection, poisoned traces, compromised baselines, dependency updates, tampered
receipts, or tool outputs that are syntactically plausible but semantically
misleading. Environment design therefore includes integrity checks for prompts,
dependencies, artifacts, baselines, and logs, not only better proxy metrics.

The clearest illustration comes from the field that named the failure.

::: {.callout-field-note title="The boat that won by crashing"}
In a boat-racing game used to study reinforcement learning, an agent rewarded on game score stopped racing and looped in a lagoon to harvest respawning targets, catching fire and never finishing the race while out-scoring the average human player by about 20 percent [@ClarkAmodei2016FaultyReward]. Nothing malfunctioned. The loop optimized exactly the number it was given, and that number was a proxy for "win the race" that the race never actually checked.

**Lesson:** An architecture environment may expose a scalar objective, but it must also preserve the observations, invalid-action checks, baselines, and stronger rejection gates that keep the proxy from becoming the architecture claim.
:::

Environment design should therefore include red-team checks[^fn-ml-red-teaming] for the feedback
source itself. A simulator-backed loop should record warm-up policy, execution-
versus trace-driven mode, random seeds, sampled regions, versioned workloads,
configuration files, and unsupported states. It should also include rejection
tests that look for proxy gaming, such as cross-checks against another model, sanity
constraints on bandwidth and latency, sensitivity studies, invalid-action
logs, baseline replay, and escalation to stronger fidelity when the result is
surprising or high commitment. The goal is not to distrust simulation. The
goal is to make the simulator's authority explicit.

[^fn-ml-red-teaming]: Red-teaming involves intentionally attacking a system to expose vulnerabilities, flaws, and failure modes before deployment. Here the target is the environment's own feedback source, so the loop probes a simulator or proxy for gameable corners and unsupported states before it trusts a result.

## Building Environments for New Subfields

Making that authority explicit is also the first move in building an
environment from scratch. A useful Architecture 2.0 environment for an
AI-assisted loop can start small, one bounded task where an automated tool or method can
propose actions, receive observations, hit explicit invalid-action rules, and
leave rejection reasons for review. The point is not to
build a universal hardware-design platform. The point is to choose a bounded
task where actions, observations, constraints, and rejection can be stated
cleanly.

The steps below make that tool path usable inside the bounded architecture
study defined in @sec-architecture-20-ontology.

**Environment recipe.** Constructing a small, bounded, rejectable Architecture 2.0 environment proceeds step by step.

1. Define the task in architectural language, such as workload characterization, cache exploration, accelerator parameter search, compiler/runtime tuning, chiplet partitioning, reliability analysis, or design-review critique.
2. Choose the representation the loop will read and write, such as a configuration file, architecture description, graph, trace record, report, RTL fragment, test bench, or design-loop card.
3. Define the action schema by stating which fields can change, which are read-only, which combinations are legal, and which changes require human approval.
4. Wrap the tool path, such as a simulator, compiler, profiler, RTL flow, EDA stage, runtime system, benchmark harness, or telemetry pipeline.
5. Define observations and feedback, including metrics, traces, logs, warnings, errors, generated artifacts, cost, and fidelity level.
6. Define action and outcome semantics. Illegal parameters, candidate-caused noncompilable or nonsynthesizable artifacts, violated constraints, and unsupported workloads may invalidate a candidate. Timeouts, simulator crashes, and setup failures remain environment failures unless a declared rule assigns the candidate responsibility.
7. Log provenance and failure records, preserving tool versions, seeds, workload versions, failed runs, rejected alternatives, and reasons for each disposition.
8. State the accountable decision rule, defining what the architect reviews, what can be accepted automatically, and what must escalate.

Once built, the readiness test for this environment is simple. Could a second method, student, or research group
act inside the same harness without private knowledge from the original author?
Could a rejected candidate remain understandable six months later after tool
versions, workload revisions, and scripts have changed? If the answer is no,
the project may still contain a useful wrapper, but it has not yet produced a
durable Architecture 2.0 environment.

The eight-step recipe is what
keeps Architecture 2.0 from becoming prompt-to-chip rhetoric. The first useful
environment for a new subfield is often narrow. It typically includes a bounded workload, a small
action space, a clear simulator wrapper, and a disciplined log of failures.
That is enough to show the loop what it can try, what it can observe, and
what evidence matters.

Applying this operational recipe, the lighthouse prompt suggests a good starting point. Instead of trying to
build an end-to-end processor designer, start with an XRBench workload slice
and a small set of candidate architectural knobs, such as vector width, local memory,
cache size, data layout, or accelerator interface. Define which candidates are
invalid, which feedback is cheap, which feedback is expensive, and what
evidence would be needed to move from proxy exploration to simulator or
synthesis-backed claims. That is a real Architecture 2.0 environment even if
it is far from a complete architecture design system.

The same abstraction scales down as readily as it scales up. A single hardware
prefetcher makes a perfectly good environment. Its actions are prefetch depth
and pattern, its cheap feedback is cache hit rate and memory bandwidth from a
cycle-level simulator, and its rejection rule is any configuration that breaches
a bandwidth or correctness bound. The environment idea is not tied to the size
of the artifact; it is tied to having legal actions, observable feedback, and
something that can say no.
It is complete only after it names the workload trace, simulator version, failed
configurations, proxy limits, and the point at which a human escalates from
hit-rate evidence to correctness or traffic review.

::: {#pri-tools-environments .callout-design-principle title="Turn tools into environments"}
A tool wrapper is credible only when it defines legal actions, observations,
costs, invalid states, and failure paths, then preserves those meanings as
workloads, tools, and action schemas change. Version the harness and retain its
provenance. A command that returns a number is not yet an environment a loop
can act in.
:::

## Environment Validity and Lifecycle {#sec-archops-operating-discipline}

An environment is useful only if it preserves the semantics of the
architectural question. If the workload is wrong, the action space omits the
important decision, the simulator hides a constraint, the proxy is
uncalibrated, or the logging drops failed candidates, the loop may become more
efficient at producing weak evidence.

To maintain this semantic link, environment validity is checked along the
contract fields that can go stale, six layers in practice:

- **Workload layer:** The inputs, distributions, and software stack match the
  intended use.
- **Action layer:** The loop can change the right architectural variables
  without violating hidden constraints.
- **Observation layer:** Returned metrics are meaningful and labeled with their
  source and valid scope.
- **Feedback-cost layer:** Cost and latency are visible before the loop commits
  to the next evaluation.
- **Provenance layer:** Recorded tool versions, inputs, assumptions, and outputs
  support audit. Replay additionally requires executable bindings to the
  declared environment and procedure.
- **Outcome layer:** Illegal or unsupported candidates remain distinct from
  timeouts, tool crashes, setup failures, and inconclusive runs.

The checkpoint below turns these layers into a practical trust test.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Environment Trust Gate"}
Before trusting an environment's verdict on an AI-generated candidate, confirm:

- the workload, action space, and observations still match the architecture question the loop was intended to solve;
- observation sources, their valid scope, and the cost and latency of another evaluation are explicit;
- versions, assumptions, and rejected candidates are recorded;
- candidate failures are distinguished from tool, setup, and harness failures;
- another team could deploy an automated optimizer in this harness (the readiness test above).
:::

Ordinary lifecycle practice keeps those layers aligned over time. Versioning,
monitoring, drift detection, rollback, and ownership records are familiar from
MLOps[^fn-systems-mlops], benchmark governance, and reliable service operation.
Applied here, they keep an environment's workloads, tools, action schemas, and
outcome meanings from changing silently after the first paper or release.

[^fn-systems-mlops]: MLOps maintains deployed learning systems through practices such as versioning, monitoring, drift detection, rollback, and ownership records [@SculleyEtAl2015Hidden; @BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. The same practices help keep architecture harnesses comparable as their workloads and tools change.

The lifecycle work can be stated in operational terms
(@tbl-archops-operating-discipline). The table is intentionally prosaic because
ordinary drift must become visible before it changes what an observation means.

| **Drift source** | **What to record** | **Failure if ignored** | **Operating response** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Workload and benchmark updates | Versions, scenarios, excluded inputs, telemetry shifts, and coverage changes. | A loop keeps optimizing a stale or unrepresentative slice. | Re-run coverage checks and weaken or invalidate affected claims. |
| Tool and model changes | Simulator, compiler, EDA, model, prompt, policy, dependency, seed, configuration, and attestation versions. | Results become incomparable across runs without a clear cause, or a changed dependency silently alters the evidence source. | Treat version changes as evidence events and preserve old receipts. |
| Action-space changes | New knobs, deprecated knobs, invalid combinations, and permission boundaries. | A method proposes actions the environment no longer supports. | Update validity checks and record invalid-action traces. |
| Observation and review rules | Fidelity labels, escalation thresholds, signoff checks, waivers, and reviewer decisions. | A proxy keeps being used after the claim has moved beyond its validated scope. | Recalibrate the rule and require review appropriate to the new claim. |
| Accountable ownership | Decision owner, review date, redaction boundary, and escalation path. | Accountability disappears into scripts, tools, or automated systems. | Keep commitment decisions explicit and auditable. |

: **Maintaining the environment preserves loop validity over time:** The lifecycle practice is to
version workloads, tools, actions, observation and review rules, and ownership so that drift
does not silently change what a loop result means. {#tbl-archops-operating-discipline tbl-colwidths="[18,25,25,22]"}

Read down the failure column and the rows share one shape. Something changed,
nothing recorded it, and a loop result quietly stopped meaning what a claim
assumed it meant. The operating responses answer in kind, treating each change
as something to record and re-check rather than as background noise, and those
mundane records are what keep a long-lived harness comparable over time.

## Open Research Questions

The concepts in this chapter establish the foundation for AI-assisted
architecture environments. The open questions here are not generic wrapper
engineering. They ask how tools become valid action spaces, interpretable
observation sources, and explicit failure paths for architecture work.

1. **What must an architecture environment contract expose before a loop can act?**
An environment contract should name the task boundary, legal actions,
observations, constraints, feedback types, invalid-action semantics, provenance
and integrity records, and stop or review paths. A strong
contribution would define this contract for a simulator, compiler, commercial
EDA flow, or deployment setting and test whether it passes the readiness test
above with an independent method. The fields in @tbl-environment-contract
give the concrete starting point.

2. **How can legacy tool traces suggest environment contracts?**
Legacy EDA flows and simulators emit warnings, timeouts, partial artifacts, and
unstructured logs rather than clean environment APIs. A log-to-contract system
could observe tool interactions and propose action schemas, constraint bounds,
invalid-action labels, and stop conditions. The evaluation should measure
whether the inferred contract predicts legal actions and failure modes on
held-out tool runs, and whether human reviewers can correct the contract
without rewriting the wrapper from scratch.

3. **How should environments classify invalid actions and rejection paths?**
A loop must know the difference between an illegal action, a malformed input, a
tool failure, a proxy warning, a real architectural rejection, and an
inconclusive run. A research contribution would define an invalid-action
outcome taxonomy for architecture environments and test whether it prevents
optimizers from exploiting wrapper gaps. The evaluation should report false
passes, false rejects, repairable failures, and cases that require human
review.

4. **How can negative evidence transfer across simulated and physical tool flows?**
Positive architectural wins are highly context-dependent, but failures such as
routing congestion, IR drop, timing violations, and design-rule failures often
expose reusable physical limits. A thesis-level contribution would define a
redacted failure-transfer format between high-fidelity physical flows and
faster proxy environments, then test whether transferred constraints improve
early rejection on different workloads or technology nodes. The difficult part
is showing useful transfer without treating one physical failure as a universal
rule. The EDA-stage contracts in @tbl-eda-stage-contract give the concrete
setting.

5. **How do architecture environments stay valid as tools and workloads drift?**
A one-time environment can support a paper, but a maintained environment is
needed for a field. A concrete systems paper would build a lifecycle monitor
that detects changes in workloads, tools, models, or runtime assumptions, then
dispatches revalidation jobs for claims whose evidence may no longer apply. The
contribution should be judged by whether it correctly maintains, weakens, or
rejects historical claims under human-defined review gates, extending the
lifecycle practice described above.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked what turns a tool flow into an environment a generative model
can act in and a reviewer can trust. In Architecture 1.0, simulators, compilers,
profilers, RTL flows, and deployment logs sit behind the architect as ways to
gather evidence. In Architecture 2.0 they have to move inside the loop and
become explicit environments, places that define legal actions, return
meaningful observations, price each evaluation, carry constraints and
provenance, and route illegal, unsupported, or under-evidenced candidates to a
declared stop or review path. That is the whole content of turning a tool into
an environment.

An environment earns trust only while it still preserves the architectural question. If
the workload is unrepresentative, the action space omits the real decision, the
proxy is uncalibrated, or failed candidates go unlogged, the loop just becomes
more efficient at producing weak evidence. Validity therefore has to be checked
layer by layer, across workload, action, observation, cost, provenance, and
outcome semantics, before an environment's verdict is worth believing.

The second, easily missed half is maintenance. Workloads drift, tools change,
benchmarks gain rules, and models learn stale assumptions, so a one-time
environment and a maintained one look identical on the first run and diverge
by the tenth. Ordinary lifecycle practice versions workloads, tools, and action
schemas, preserves provenance and failure records, and reruns validity checks
when those inputs change. Building the environment may support one paper.
Maintaining it lets later users know whether the harness still means the same
thing.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** An environment is a contract over legal actions, returned observations, provenance, and failure paths. That contract lets another method act in the same harness.
- **Reader test:** Could another method act in the same harness without private knowledge from the original author?
- **Up next:** Once the environment defines what action and feedback mean, @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization can ask which methods belong inside the loop.
:::
